---
name: recap-the-session
description: 'Close a thinking session with a short, honest recap: the decisions made, the risks and gaps surfaced, the questions still open, and the natural next step. Use at the end of a clarifying, user-experience, design, or planning session so the value is legible and the next stage knows what to carry forward.'
---

# Recap the session

A session is only useful if what it produced is easy to see and act on. Before you
finish, pull the thread together into a short recap the human can scan in a few
seconds. This is a lightweight close-out, not a second artifact — keep it tight.

## What the recap covers

Include only the lines that carry real content; drop the rest.

- **Decisions made** — what got settled in this session. Point to where it came
  from when it rests on source material.
- **Risks and gaps** — what looks risky, thin, or unverified.
- **Still open** — the questions that remain for the human or an SME.
- **Next step** — the natural next stage and what to carry into it, when there is
  an obvious one.

## How to do it

- Keep it short. A handful of bullets, not a report. Match its weight to the
  change (see calibrate-scrutiny).
- Recap only what actually happened. Don't invent a decision to fill a section,
  and don't restate the whole artifact.
- Keep facts, inferences, and open questions distinguishable (see
  separate-fact-from-inference).
- If nothing was really settled, say so plainly. "No decision reached yet" is a
  useful recap.

## Point to the next stage

A common path runs Clarify Change → Define User Experience or Design Doc Assistant
→ Documentation Planner → Draft Documentation. Use it as a guide, not a script:
name the stage this change actually needs next, and say what the next stage should
carry forward — the brief, the decisions, or the open questions. If the work is
done, say that instead of inventing a next step.

The recap is what the human remembers after the session closes. Make it worth
remembering.
